title: Chmod Calculator: Unix File Permissions Made Easy
description: What does chmod 755 actually do? Most developers get this one crucial setting wrong.

schemas.headline: Chmod Calculator: Unix File Permissions Made Easy
schemas.description: What does chmod 755 actually do? Most developers get this one crucial setting wrong. Use our interactive calculator to secure your files instantly before your next deployment.

mainEntity.name: What is a chmod calculator used for?

acceptedAnswer.text: A chmod calculator is a tool that helps you visually determine the correct numerical or symbolic permissions for files and directories in Unix and Linux systems. Instead of calculating octal values (like 755 or 644) manually, you can simply check boxes for read, write, and execute access for the owner, group, and other users.

The meaning of chmod 755 is that the owner has full read, write, and execute permissions (7), while the group and all other users have read and execute permissions only (5). This is the standard and recommended permission setting for directories and executable scripts on web servers.

mainEntity.name: How do linux file permissions work?

acceptedAnswer.text: Linux file permissions are divided into three categories of users: the Owner, the Group, and Others (everyone else). For each category, you can assign Read (r=4), Write (w=2), and Execute (x=1) permissions. Adding these numbers together gives you the octal permission digit for that category.

mainEntity.name: How do I use the chmod command?

acceptedAnswer.text: The chmod command is used in the terminal to change file permissions. The basic syntax is 'chmod [permissions] [filename]'. For example, 'chmod 644 index.html' gives the owner read/write access and everyone else read-only access. You can use either octal numbers (like 644) or symbolic representation (like u+rw,go+r).

mainEntity.name: What are setuid, setgid, and sticky bit in chmod?

acceptedAnswer.text: These are special permission bits. Setuid (4) allows a file to be executed with the privileges of its owner. Setgid (2) applies to directories so that new files created within inherit the directory's group, or allows files to be executed with the group's privileges. The sticky bit (1) on a directory prevents users from deleting files they do not own, commonly used on the /tmp directory.
